abstract:
STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering, and Mathematics) education has emerged as a transformative pedagogical framework essential for fostering 21st-century competencies and driving innovation. This study employs a systematic literature review, utilizing the PRISMA guidelines adapted from Sharif and Moher et al., to analyze the evolving landscape of STEM and STEAM education research within the Indonesian context. A total of 63 articles were selected based on rigorous inclusion criteria and evaluated using a Paper Classification Form (PCF) adapted from Kizilaslan et al. The analysis categorized the literature across several dimensions, including publication year, research methodology, data collection techniques, disciplinary focus, and target demographics.

The results reveal that research in Indonesia is predominantly centered on learning strategies employed within secondary school science curricula. Experimental designs occupy a significant portion of the methodological landscape, with Project-Based Learning (PjBL), Problem-Based Learning (PBL), and Inquiry-based approaches emerging as the most frequent instructional models integrated with STEM/STEAM frameworks. Furthermore, the analysis identifies critical thinking skills, scientific literacy, and student learning outcomes as the primary thematic concerns of current scholarship. The study concludes by highlighting the necessity for more robust empirical research to fully realize the potential of STEAM education in equipping students with the complex skills required for the modern era.
